questions.prompt: Two siblings are taking a math quiz. The probability that the older sibling will solve the quiz correctly is 2/5, and the probability that the younger sibling will solve it is 3/7. What is the probability that at least one of them will solve the quiz?
questions.solution: Use the principle of inclusion-exclusion, which is a simplification of the law of total probability: P (A ∪ B) = P(A) + P(B) - P (A ∩ B) Where: P (A ∪ B) is the probability that at least one of them solves the quiz. P(A) is the probability that the older sibling solves the quiz (2/5). P(B) is the probability that the younger sibling solves the quiz (3/7). P (A ∩ B) is the probability that both of them solve the quiz. To find P (A ∩ B), we\'ll assume that the events are independent (which is a common assumption in such problems) P (A ∩ B) = P(A) X P(B) P (A ∩ B) = (2/5) X (3/7) =6/35 Now, substitute this value back into the equation P (A ∪ B) = P(A) + P(B) - P (A ∩ B) $$P\ (A\  \cup \ B) = \ \frac{2}{5}\  + \ \frac{3}{7}\  - \ \frac{6}{35} = \ \frac{29}{35} - \ \frac{6}{35} = \ \frac{23}{35}$$ Another approach P(A) = Probability that the older sibling solves the quiz (2/5). P(B) = Probability that the younger sibling solves the quiz (3/7). The probability that neither of them solves the quiz is the complement of at least one of them solving: P (neither A nor B) = P (A\' and B\'). Now, we can find the complement probabilities: P(A\') is the probability that the older sibling does not solve the quiz, which is 1 - P(A) = 1 - 2/5 = 3/5. P(B\') is the probability that the younger sibling does not solve the quiz, which is 1 - P(B) = 1 - 3/7 = 4/7. Now, we can find P (neither A nor B) using the probability of the intersection: P (neither A nor B) = P (A\' and B\') = P(A\') x P(B\') = (3/5) x (4/7) =12/35 To find the probability that at least one of them solves the quiz (P (A ∪ B)), we subtract the probability of neither of them solving from 1: P (A ∪ B) = 1 - P (neither A nor B) = 1 - 12/35. Now, calculate this probability: P (A ∪ B) = 1 - 12/35 = 35/35 - 12/35 = 23/35.

questions.prompt: In a basketball game, two players have free throw shooting percentages. Player A has a 75% success rate, and Player B has a 60% success rate. What is the probability that at least one of them will make a free throw?
questions.solution: Let\'s denote: P(A): The probability that Player A makes a free throw, which is 75% or 0.75. P(B): The probability that Player B makes a free throw, which is 60% or 0.60. Now, we want to find the probability that both players miss (neither A nor B makes a free throw). To do this, we\'ll multiply their probabilities of missing: P (neither A nor B) = (1 - P(A)) x (1 - P(B)) P (neither A nor B) = (1 - 0.75) x (1 - 0.60) P (neither A nor B) = 0.25 x 0.40 Now, calculate P (neither A nor B): P (neither A nor B) = 0.10 To find the probability that at least one of them makes a free throw, we subtract the probability of both players missing from 1: P (at least one makes a free throw) = 1 - P (neither A nor B) P (at least one makes a free throw) = 1 - 0.10 P (at least one makes a free throw) = 0.90 So, the probability that at least one of the two players will make a free throw is 0.90. Or P (A ∪ B) = P(A) + P(B) - P (A ∩ B) $$P\ (A\  \cup \ B) = \ 0.6\  + \ 0.75\  - (0.6 \times 0.75) = \ 0.9$$

questions.prompt: that Machine X will produce a defect-free product is 4/5, and the probability that Machine Y will do so is 3/4. What is the probability that at least one of the machines will produce a defect-free product? **Solution** Let\'s denote: P(X): The probability that Machine X produces a defect-free product, which is 4/5. P(Y): The probability that Machine Y produces a defect-free product, which is 3/4. Now, we want to find the probability that both machines produce defective products (neither X nor Y produces a defect-free product). To do this, we\'ll multiply their probabilities of producing defective products: P (neither X nor Y) = (1 - P(X)) X (1 - P(Y)) P (neither X nor Y) = (1 - 4/5) X (1 - 3/4) P (neither X nor Y) = (1/5) X (1/4) Now, calculate P (neither X nor Y): P (neither X nor Y) = (1/5) X (1/4) = 1/20 To find the probability that at least one of the machines produces a defect-free product, we subtract the probability of both machines producing defective products from 1: P (at least one produces defect-free) = 1 - P (neither X nor Y) P (at least one produces defect-free) = 1 - 1/20 Now, calculate this probability: P (at least one produces defect-free) = 20/20 - 1/20 = 19/20 **Or** P (A ∪ B) = P(A) + P(B) - P (A ∩ B) $$P\ (A\  \cup \ B) = \frac{4}{5}\  + \frac{3}{4}\  - \left( \frac{4}{5} \times \frac{3}{4} \right) = \frac{19}{20} = 0.95$$
Calculate the probability of getting exactly two heads in three-coin tosses.
questions.solution: Method 1 S = {HHH, HTT, THT, TTH, TTT, THH, HTH, HHT} P (exactly two heads) =3/8 Method 2 To calculate the probability of getting exactly two heads in three-coin tosses, you can use the binomial probability formula. In this case, the probability of getting a head (H) on a fair coin toss is 0.5, and the probability of getting a tail (T) is also 0.5. The binomial probability formula is: $$P(X = k) = c(n,k)\ p^{k}{(1 - p)}^{n - k}$$ P(X=k) is the probability of getting exactly n is the number of trials (in this case, three-coin tosses). k is the number of successes you want (in this case, two heads). p is the probability of success on a single trial (in this case, the probability of getting a head, which is 0.5). $c(n,k)$ the binomial coefficient, which represents the number of ways to choose k successes out of n trials and is calculated as $c(n,k) = \frac{n!}{k!(n - k)!}$ Let\'s calculate the probability: $$P(X = 2) = c(3,2)\ {0.5}^{2}{(1 - 0.5)}^{3 - 2}$$ $$P(X = 2) = 3\ {0.5}^{2}{(1 - 0.5)}^{3 - 2} = 0.375$$ Therefore, the probability of getting exactly two heads in three-coin tosses is 0.375, or 37.5%.

questions.prompt: concrete for a series of foundation columns. The company has a concrete mixture with a known defect rate of 5%, meaning that 5% of the foundation columns poured will have defects. The company plans to pour 20 foundation columns for this project. Using the binomial distribution, calculate the probability that exactly 2 of the 20 poured foundation columns will have defects.
questions.solution: use the binomial distribution formula to find the probability of success (a defect) and failure (no defect) for each column, and then calculate the probability of exactly 2 defects out of 20 trials. P(X=k) is the probability of getting exactly k successes (in this case, k defects) n is the number of trials (in this case, the number of poured foundation columns, which is 20). k is the number of successes we\'re interested in (in this case, 2 defects). p is the probability of success on a single trial (in this case, the probability of a defect, which is 5% or 0.05) $c(n,k)$ the binomial coefficient, which represents the number of ways to choose k successes out of n trials and is calculated as $c(n,k) = \frac{n!}{k!(n - k)!}$ Let\'s calculate the probability: $$P(X = 2) = c(20,2)\ {0.05}^{2}{(1 - 0.05)}^{20 - 2}$$ $$P(X = 2) = 190\ \  \times {0.05}^{2}{(1 - 0.05)}^{18}$$ $$P(X = 2) = 190\ \  \times {0.05}^{2}{(1 - 0.05)}^{18} = 190 \times 0.0025 \times 0.358491 = 0.17028355$$ So, the probability of exactly 2 defects out of 20 poured foundation columns is approximately 0.1703

questions.prompt: In a construction project, you need to assess the probability of meeting the quality standards for concrete strength. You have collected 10 concrete samples from different batches and tested their compressive strength. The project specifications require that at least 7 out of the 10 samples have a minimum strength of 35 MPa to meet the quality standards. The strengths of the samples are as follows (in MPa): 32, 36, 38, 34, 35, 36, 37, 33, 31, and 35. Calculate the probability of a single sample having a strength greater than or equal to 35 MPa
questions.solution: Step 1: Calculate the probability of each sample having a strength less than 35 MPa. You provided the strengths of the samples as follows (in MPa): 32, 36, 38, 34, 35, 36, 37, 33, 31, and 35. Out of these, samples with strengths less than 35 MPa are: 32, 34, 33, 31. So, the probability of a single sample having a strength less than 35 MPa is $$P\ (sample\  < \ 35\ MPa) = \frac{4}{10}$$ Step 2: Calculate the probability of a single sample having a strength greater than or equal to 35 MPa. This is the complement of the probability calculated in Step 1: $$P\ (sample\  > \ 35\ MPa) = 1 - P\ (sample\  < \ 35\ MPa) = 1 - \frac{4}{10} = 0.6$$

questions.prompt: In a construction project, you need to assess the probability of meeting the quality standards for concrete strength. You have collected 10 concrete samples from different batches and tested their compressive strength. The project specifications require that at least 7 out of the 10 samples have a minimum strength of 35 MPa to meet the quality standards. The strengths of the samples are as follows (in MPa): 32, 36, 38, 34, 35, 36, 37, 33, 31, and 35. what is the probability that you randomly select 3 of them consecutively (without replacement) and they all have strengths greater than 35 MPa?
questions.solution: Given that there are 6 concrete samples meeting the minimum strength requirement, Step 1: Calculate the probability of selecting the first sample with a strength greater than 35 MPa. Since there are 6 samples meeting the requirement, the probability of this is 6/10. $P(A) = \frac{6}{10}$ Step 2: Calculate the probability of selecting the second sample with a strength greater than 35 MPa. After the first selection, there are now 9 samples remaining, and 5 of them still meet the requirement. So, the probability is 5/9. $P(B) = \frac{5}{9}$ Step 3: Calculate the probability of selecting the third sample with a strength greater than 35 MPa. After the second selection, there are 8 samples remaining, and 4 of them still meet the requirement. So, the probability is 4/8. $P(C) = \frac{4}{10}$ $P(A) \times P(B) \times P(C) = \frac{6}{10} \times \frac{5}{9} \times \frac{4}{8} = \frac{5}{54}$

questions.prompt: In a construction project, you need to assess the probability of meeting the quality standards for concrete strength. The strengths of the samples are as follows (in MPa): 32, 36, 38, 34, 35, 36, 37, 33, 31, and 35.
questions.solution: Given that a single sample has a 35 MPa strength with a probability of 0.6 (meeting the standards), the probability of a single sample not meeting the standards is $$P(A) = \ 1 - \frac{6}{10} = 0.4\ $$ Since each sample is independent, we can calculate the probability that none of the 10 samples meet the standards by raising the probability of a single sample not meeting the standards to the power of 10 (as there are 10 samples): $$P(None\ meet\ standards) = (0.4)^{10}$$

questions.prompt: In a construction project, you need to assess the probability of meeting the quality standards for concrete strength. questions.solution: Probability of selecting 7 samples meeting the requirement out of 20 samples: $$c(n,r) = \frac{n!}{r!(n - r)!} = \frac{20!}{7!(20 - 7)!}$$ $$c(20,7) = \frac{20!}{7!(20 - 7)!}$$

questions.prompt: 10 freeman and 15 sophomores? In how many ways can 3 freeman and 5 sophomores be selected? In how many ways can a committee with exactly 1 freeman be selected? In how many ways can a committee with at least 1 freeman be selected?

questions.prompt: probability of heads appearing at least one time?
questions.solution: The possible outcomes for each coin toss are either heads (H) or tails (T). When you toss a fair coin, each outcome is equally likely, so there is 2\^4 = 16 possible outcomes in total. These outcomes can be listed as follows: S = {HHHH, HHHT, HHTH, HTHH, THHH, HHTT, HTTH, TTHH, HTHT, THTH, THTT, TTTH, TTTT, TTTT, TTHT, HTTT} Assume A is the event of not getting any heads, and B is the event (at least one head) P (A) not getting any heads $= \ P(TTTT)\  = \ (1/2)\  \times \ (1/2)\  \times \ (1/2)\  \times \ (1/2)\  = \ 1/16$ Using the complement rule, the probability of getting at least one head is: P(B) at least one head = 1 - P (not getting any heads) $$P(B) = 1 - \ P\ (A) = = \ 1\  - \ 1/16\  = \ 15/16$$

questions.prompt: What's the value of the constant (c) that makes $f(x) = \frac{x^{3}}{4}$ a valid probability density function?
questions.solution: To verify that $f(x)$ is a PDF, it needs to meet two conditions 1)  f(x) is always positive $f(x) > 0$ 2)  And the area under the curve is equal to 1 $P(a \leq X \leq b = \int_{a}^{b}{f(x)\ dx} = 1$ $$\int_{0}^{c}{f(x)\ dx} = \int_{0}^{c}{\frac{x^{3}}{4}\ dx = \left\lbrack \frac{x^{4}}{16} \right\rbrack_{0}^{c}} = \left\lbrack \frac{c^{4}}{16} - 0 \right\rbrack = 1$$ $$c^{4} = 16 \rightarrow c = \mp 2$$ For $f(x) > 0\ \  \rightarrow c = + 2\$

questions.prompt: For X a continuous random variable $f(x) = \sqrt{x^{2}}\ \$ What's the probability that X falls between -1 and 1?
questions.solution: $$f(x) = \sqrt{x^{2}} = |x| = \left\{ \begin{array}{r} x\ \ \ \ \ :\ \ \ x \geq 0 \\ - x\ \ \ \ \ :\ \ \ x \leq 0 \end{array} \right\}$$ $$\int_{- 1}^{1}{f(x)\ dx} = \int_{- 1}^{0}{f(x)\ dx} + \int_{0}^{1}{f(x)\ dx = \int_{- 1}^{0}{( - x)\ dx} + \int_{0}^{1}{(x)\ dx}}$$ $$\int_{- 1/2}^{1}{f(x)\ dx} = \left\lbrack - \frac{x^{2}}{2} \right\rbrack_{- 1}^{0} + \left\lbrack \frac{x^{2}}{2} \right\rbrack_{0}^{1} = \left( \frac{- 1}{2} \right) + \left( \frac{1}{2} \right) = 0$$ $$P( - 1 \leq X \leq 1 = \int_{- 1}^{1}{f(x)\ dx} = 0$$

questions.prompt: For X a continuous random variable with PDF given by $f(x)$ What's the probability that X falls between (-0.5 and 1.5)? $$f(x) = \left\{ \begin{array}{r} 1 + x\ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ x \leq 1 \\ 1 - x\ \ \ \ \ \ \  - 1 \leq x \leq 0 \\ x^{2}\ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ 0 \leq x \leq 2 \\ 2 + x\ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ x > 2 \end{array} \right\}$$
questions.solution: $$P\left( \frac{- 1}{2} \leq X \leq \frac{3}{2} \right) = \int_{\frac{- 1}{2}}^{0}{(1 - x)dx} + \int_{0}^{3/2}{\left( x^{2} \right)dx}$$ $$P\left( \frac{- 1}{2} \leq X \leq \frac{3}{2} \right) = \left\lbrack x - \frac{x^{2}}{2} \right\rbrack_{- 1/2}^{0} + \left\lbrack \frac{x^{3}}{3} \right\rbrack_{1}^{3/2} = \left( \frac{- 5}{8} \right) + \left( \frac{27}{24} \right) = \frac{1}{2}$$

questions.prompt: You are analyzing the test results of 50 concrete samples, each of which has been tested to measure its compressive strength. questions.solution: Calculate the Z- score for X=26 $$Z = \frac{X - \mu}{\sigma} = \frac{26 - 24}{2} = 1$$ Use a standard normal distribution table or calculator to find the probability associated with the z-score. In this case, you are looking for P(Z\>1) $$P(X > 26) = P(Z > 1) = 0.8413$$ Keep in mind that the Z- chart always gives the probability to the left of Z-score, since the area under the entire distribution is always equal to 1 and the area to the left is (0.8413), therefore, to get the area to the right $$1 - 0.8413 = 0.1587$$

questions.prompt: You are considering taking a job that offers a salary of \$50,000 with a 60% chance of a \$5,000 bonus and a 40% chance of no bonus. Calculate the expected total income for this job.
questions.solution: To calculate the expected total income for the job, which includes the base salary and the potential bonus, you can use the concept of expected value. [Base Salary]{.underline}: The base salary is \$50,000. [Bonus:]{.underline} Probability of receiving a \$5,000 bonus (60%): 0.60 Probability of not receiving a bonus (40%): 0.40 Amount of the bonus: \$5,000 Now, calculate the expected bonus: *\* $$E\lbrack X\rbrack = \sum_{i = 1}^{i = k}x_{i}p_{i}$$ Expected Bonus = (0.60 x times \$5,000) + (0.40 x times \$0) = \$3,000 [Total Income:]{.underline} Now, add the base salary and the expected bonus to calculate the expected total income: Expected Total Income = Base Salary + Expected Bonus = \$50,000 + \$3,000 = \$53,000

questions.prompt: You are considering two investment options. Option A has a 60% chance of a 10% return and a 40% chance of a 5% loss. Option B has a 70% chance of a 7% return and a 30% chance of a 3% loss. Calculate the expected return for each option.
questions.solution: The expected return is a measure of the average return you can expect from an investment. Here\'s how you can calculate it for both options: **[Option A]{.underline}**: Probability of a 10% return (60%): 0.60 Probability of a 5% loss (40%): 0.40 Return for a 10% gain: 10% Return for a 5% loss: -5% (negative because it\'s a loss) Now, calculate the expected return for Option A: Expected Return for Option A = (0.60 X times 0.10) + (0.40 X times (-0.05)) = 0.06 - 0.02 = 0.04 or 4% **[Option B]{.underline}**: Probability of a 7% return (70%): 0.70 Probability of a 3% loss (30%): 0.30 Return for a 7% gain: 7% Return for a 3% loss: -3% (negative because it\'s a loss) Now, calculate the expected return for Option B: Expected Return for Option B = (0.70 X times 0.07) + (0.30 X times (-0.03)) = 0.049- 0.009 = 0.04 or 4%} Both Option A and Option B have an expected return of 4%.

questions.prompt: volume of 0.66 cubic feet, has a specific gravity of 2.65. The degree of saturation is most nearly:
questions.solution: Required $$S = \frac{w.G_{s}}{e}$$ Step 1: Determine the Total unit weight $$\gamma = \frac{W}{V} = \frac{72.4\ }{0.66\ } = 109.7\ lb/ft3$$ Step 2: Determine the dry unit weight $$\gamma_{d} = \frac{G_{s}.\gamma_{w}}{1 + e} = \frac{\gamma}{1 + w} = \frac{109.7\ }{1 + 0.19} = 91.6\ lb/ft3$$ Step 3: Determine the void ratio $$\gamma_{d} = \frac{G_{s}.\gamma_{w}}{1 + e}$$ $$91.6 = \frac{2.65 \times 62.4}{1 + e} \rightarrow e = 0.80$$ Step 3: Determine the Degree of saturation (%) $$S = \frac{w.G_{s}}{e} = \frac{0.19 \times 2.65\ }{0.8\ } = 62.93\ \%\  \approx 63\%$$

questions.prompt: An undisturbed sample of soil has a specific gravity of solids of 2.70, a moisture content 10.5%, and a void ratio of 0.63, The degree of saturation is most nearly:

moisture content of 15% and unit weight of $18.84\ KN/m^{3}$. Laboratory tests on a dried sample indicated values of $e_{\min} = 0.5\$and $e_{\max} = 0.85$ for the densest and loosest states respectively. Compute the degree of saturation and relative density assume $Gs = 2.65$
questions.solution: Required $$S = \frac{w.G_{s}}{e}$$ $$D_{r} = \frac{e_{\max} - e}{e_{\max} - e_{\min}} \times 100\%$$ Step 1: Determine the dry unit weight $$\gamma_{d} = \frac{G_{s}.\gamma_{w}}{1 + e} = \frac{\gamma}{1 + w} = \frac{18.84 \times 6.36\ }{1 + 0.15} = 104.19\ lb/ft3$$ > $$\frac{KN}{m^{3}} = 6.36\frac{lb}{ft3}$$ Step 2: Determine the void ratio $$\gamma_{d} = \frac{G_{s}.\gamma_{w}}{1 + e}$$ $$104.19 = \frac{2.65 \times 62.4}{1 + e} \rightarrow e = 0.58 > e_{\min}\ OK\ $$ Step 3: Determine the Degree of saturation (%) $$S = \frac{w.G_{s}}{e} = \frac{0.15 \times 2.65\ }{0.58\ } = 68.53\ \%\  \approx 69\%$$ Step 4: Determine the relative density $$D_{r} = \frac{e_{\max} - e}{e_{\max} - e_{\min}} = \frac{0.85 - 0.58}{0.85 - 0.5} = 77.14\% \approx 78\%$$

questions.prompt: percent. The unit weights of the sample at it densest and loosest states are respectively 114.5 and 89.1 Ib/ft3. Assume the specific gravity of the solids as $Gs = 2.64$ Determine its dry unit weight, wet unit weight when fully saturated and submerged unit weight
questions.solution: Step 1: Determine the unit weight of the solids $$G_{s} = \frac{\gamma_{s}}{\gamma_{w}} = 2.64 = \frac{\gamma_{s}}{62.4} \rightarrow \gamma_{s} = 164.74\ lb/ft3$$ $$\gamma_{s} = \frac{W_{s}}{V_{s}} \rightarrow V_{s} = \frac{114.5\ }{164.74} = 0.695$$ Step 2: Determine the void ratio when fully saturated, the volume of air is Zero and the degree of saturation is 100% $$S = \frac{V_{w}}{V_{v}}100\% \rightarrow V_{w} = V_{v}$$ $$D_{r} = \frac{\gamma_{d - field} - \gamma_{d - min}}{\gamma_{d - max} - \gamma_{d - min}}.\frac{\gamma_{d - max}}{\gamma_{d - field}} \times 100\%$$ $$0.$$ $$0.65 = \frac{\gamma_{d - field} - 89.1}{114.5 - 89.1}.\frac{114.5}{\gamma_{d - field}} \rightarrow \gamma_{d - field} = 104.11\ lb/ft3$$ Step 3: Determine the dry unit weight $$\gamma_{d} = \frac{G_{s}.\gamma_{w}}{1 + e} = \frac{2.64 \times 62.4\ }{1 + e} = 104.11\ lb/ft3 \rightarrow e = 0.58$$ Step 4: Determine the saturated unit weight $$\gamma_{sat} = \frac{\left( G_{s} + e \right)\gamma_{w}}{1 + e} = \frac{2.64 + 0.58}{1 + 0.58}62.4 = 127.16\ lb/ft3$$ Step 5: Determine the submerged (buoyant) unit weight -- Below water table $$\gamma' = \gamma_{sat} - \gamma_{w} = 127.16\  - 62.4 = 64.76$$ $$e = \frac{V_{v}}{V_{s}} = \frac{V_{A} + V_{w}}{V_{s}} = \frac{0 + V_{w}}{V_{s}} = \frac{62.4}{V_{s}} = 0.58$$

questions.prompt: Subsurface exploration indicates that a level site has a 10-ft upper layer of sand. The groundwater table is at the ground surface. The unit weight of the sand is 135.0 pcf. The effective overburden stress (psf) at a depth of l0ft is most nearly:
questions.solution: Step 1: Determine coefficient of at−rest earth pressure 𝐾𝑜 For normally consolidated soil, the 𝐾𝑜≈1−sin𝜙 $$Ko \approx 1 - sin\phi = 1 - \sin(90) = 1$$ Step 2: Determine the horizontal stress The horizontal stress, 𝜎ℎ, at depth z=10ft will equal $$\sigma_{h} = \sigma_{v} - u$$ $$\sigma_{h} = \sigma_{v}K_{0} - \gamma_{w}.h_{w}$$ $$\sigma_{h} = \gamma.h.K_{0} = 135 \times 10 \times 1 - 62.4(10) = 726\ psf$$ **Note** Unit of density: Pounds Per Cubic Feet (PCF) Unit of stress: Pound per square foot (psf) **Pcf x feet =psf** **1 psf= 47.8803 Pa** **1 psf = 0.04788 Kpa**

\sqrt{\cos^{2}\alpha - \cos^{2}\varnothing}}{cos\alpha + \sqrt{\cos^{2}\alpha - \cos^{2}\varnothing}}$$ $$K_{a} = cos20\frac{cos20 - \sqrt{\cos^{2}20 - \cos^{2}30}}{cos20 + \sqrt{\cos^{2}20 - \cos^{2}30}} = 0.441$$ Passive earth pressure coefficient $$K_{P} = cos\alpha\frac{cos\alpha\mathbf{+}\sqrt{\cos^{2}\alpha - \cos^{2}\varnothing}}{cos\alpha - \sqrt{\cos^{2}\alpha - \cos^{2}\varnothing}}$$ $$K_{a} = cos20\frac{cos20 + \sqrt{\cos^{2}20 - \cos^{2}30}}{cos20 - \sqrt{\cos^{2}20 - \cos^{2}30}} = 2.268$$ Step 2 : Calculating The active earth pressure acting on the wall $P_{a}\  = \frac{1}{2}\gamma_{s}.H^{2}K_{a} = \frac{1}{2}120\left( 8^{2} \right)(0.441) = 1693.44\ Ib/ft\$ $P_{ah} = P_{a}\ cos\alpha = (1693.44)\ cos20 = 1591.31\ Ib/ft\$ $P_{av} = P_{a}\ sin\alpha = (1693.44)\ sin20 = 580\ Ib/ft\$ $$P_{sv} = P_{s}\ \ sin\alpha = q_{s}HK_{a}sin\alpha = 731(8)(0.441)\sin(20) = 882\ Ib/ft$$ Step 3: Calculating Forces Acting on a Retaining Wall $W_{1} = \gamma_{c}.A_{c1} = 150\ (1 \times 6) = 900\ Ib/ft$ $x_{1} = 3.5\ ft$ $M_{1} = 3.15\ Kips - ft$ $P_{av} = P_{a}\ sin\alpha = (1693.44\ )\ sin20 = 580\ Ib/ft\$ $x = B = 7\ ft$ $M = 4\ Kips - ft$ $P_{sv} = P_{s}\ \ sin\alpha = q_{s}HK_{a}sin\alpha = 731(8)(0.441)\sin(20) = 882\ Ib/ft$ Step 6: Calculation of Resisting Moments Calculate the sum of the moments (about 0) of forces resisting overturning ΣMR ----------------------------------------------------------- Section    Area       Weight     Xi ( ft)       Moment (ft2)     (Ib/ft)                  (Kips-ft) --------- --------- ------------ ----------- -------------- W1         6         900          3.5          3.15 W2         3         450         2.67          1.2 W3         7         1050         3.5          3.68 W4        18         2160         5.5         11.88 W5        1.5        180           6           1.08 Pav       \-         545           7            4 Sum      **5320**                 **MR= 25** ----------------------------------------------------------- Step 4: Calculating the Overturning moment $$\sum_{}^{}\mathbf{M}_{\mathbf{0}}\mathbf{=}\mathbf{P}_{\mathbf{ah}}\left( \frac{\mathbf{H}}{\mathbf{3}} \right)\mathbf{+}\mathbf{P}_{\mathbf{sh}}\left( \frac{\mathbf{H}}{\mathbf{2}} \right)$$ $P_{ah} = P_{a}\ cos\alpha = (1693.44\ )\ cos20 = 1591.31\ Ib/ft\$ $$P_{sh} = P_{s}\ \ cos\ \alpha = q_{s}HK_{a}\ cos\ \alpha = 731(8)(0.441)\cos(20) = 2423.43\ Ib/ft$$ $$\sum_{}^{}\mathbf{M}_{\mathbf{0}} = 1591.31\ \ \left( \frac{8}{3} \right) + 2423.43\left( \frac{8}{2} \right) = 13.94\ kips - questions.prompt: The 2ft x 2ft footing shown is 2 ft below the sand\'s surface. The value of the local shear failure with a factor of safety of 3 is most nearly
questions.solution: To find the value of the bearing capacity of a local shear failure, the cohesion and angle of internal friction are reduced by two-thirds $C' = \frac{2}{3}c = 200$ and $\varnothing' = \frac{2}{3}\varnothing = 14^{0}$ Step 1: Determine the Terzaghi's Bearing Capacity Factors For soil friction angle $\varnothing' = 14^{0}$ then $N_{c} = 12.11$ , $N_{q} = 4.02$ $N_{\gamma} = 1.26$ Step 2: Determine the Terzaghi's bearing capacity For square footings, Terzaghi's general bearing capacity failure $$q_{\ u\ } = \mathbf{1.3}{c'N}_{c} + {qN}_{q} + \mathbf{0.4}\ {\gamma BN}_{\gamma}$$ $$q_{\ u\ } = 1.3(200)(12.11) + 120(2)(4.02) + 0.4(120)(2)(1.26)\mathbf{= 4234.36 \approx 4.25\ Ksf}$$

questions.prompt: You are conducting a survey to measure the elevation difference between two points, A and B. The benchmark elevation (known reference point) is 150.0 meters. You set up your leveling instrument at point A, and the height of the instrument (HI) is 1.2 meters. You take a reading at both point A and point B. Readings: Backsight Reading at Point A = 3.5 meters Foresight Reading at Point B = 2.1 meters.
questions.solution: The formula for calculating the elevation of a new benchmark from a benchmark of known elevation is **RL = Benchmark Elevation ± (Backsight Reading - Foresight Reading) ± HI** **The height of the instrument (HI)= Benchmark Elevation +Backsight Reading** 1.  Elevation (Point A) = the leveling instrument Elevation + Backsight Reading 2.  Elevation (Point B) = the leveling instrument Elevation - Foresight Reading Calculate the Reduced Level (RL) at Point A: The reduced level of any point (i) taken from the benchmark position RL at Point A = Benchmark Elevation + HI - Backsight Reading RL at Point A = 150.0 meters + 1.2 meters - 3.5 meters RL at Point A = 147.7 meters Elevation (B) = Benchmark Elevation + (Backsight Reading - Foresight Reading) Elevation (B) = 150.0 meters + (3.5 meters - 2.1 meters) Elevation (B) = 150.0 meters + 1.4 meters Elevation (Point B) = 151.4 meters Elevation (Point B) - Elevation (Point A) = 151.4 meters - 147.7 meters = 3.7 meters. Elevation difference = Benchmark Elevation + HI + Backsight Reading - Foresight Reading

questions.prompt: You are conducting a direct leveling survey to determine the elevation difference between two points, A and B. The benchmark elevation (known reference point) is 200.0 meters. You set up your leveling instrument at Point A, and the height of the instrument (HI) is 1.5 meters. You take a reading at both Point A and Point B. Given Information: Benchmark Elevation = 200.0 meters Height of Instrument (HI) at Point A = 1.5 meters Readings: Backsight Reading at Point A = 4.0 meters Foresight Reading at Point B = 3.2 meters
questions.solution: Elevation (A) = Benchmark Elevation + HI - Backsight Reading Elevation (A) = 200.0 meters + 1.5 meters - 4 meters Elevation (A) = 197.5 meters Elevation (B) = Benchmark Elevation + (Backsight Reading - Foresight Reading) Elevation (B) = 200.0 meters + (4 meters - 3.2 meters) Elevation (B) = 200.0 meters + 1.4 meters Elevation (Point B) = 200.8 meters Elevation (Point B) - Elevation (Point A) = 200.8 meters - 197.5 meters = 3.3 meters.

questions.prompt: You are conducting a survey to measure the elevation difference between two points, Point A and Point B. The benchmark elevation (known reference point) is 820 feet. The leveling instrument is set up at the halfway (Point C), After taking readings at both Point A and Point B, you determine that the elevation difference between these two points is 7.22 feet. What's the height of the instrument (HI) at point C
questions.solution: When the instrument is set up at Point C, and Point C is halfway between Point A and Point B, the Elevation (Point C) is the average of the elevations at Point A and Point B. Calculation the elevation of Point B Elevation Difference between Point A and Point B= $\mathrm{\Delta}E = E(B) - E(A)$ $$7.22 = E(B) - 820 \rightarrow E(B) = 827.22\ \ feet$$ Calculation the elevation of Point C Elevation (Point C) = (Elevation (Point A) + Elevation (Point B)) / 2 = (820+827.22)/2= 823.61 feet Or Elevation of Point C = Benchmark Elevation + (Elevation difference / 2) Elevation of Point C = 820 ft + (7.22 ft / 2.0) = = 823.61 ft The height (HI) at point C =Elevation (Point C) -the benchmark elevation =823.61-820=3.61 feet

questions.prompt: If the bearing is in the northwest quadrant, the azimuth N37°13. W. What's the azimuth
questions.solution: Azimuth angles are usually measured in a clockwise direction from north. To find the equivalent azimuth, you need to measure it from the north in a clockwise direction. Start with 360°. Subtract the degrees: 360° - 37° = 323°. Subtract the minutes: 0\' - 13\' = -13\'. Subtract the seconds: 0\" - 0\" = 0\". Now you have 323° - 13\' - 0\". But there\'s a common convention in DMS notation, which is to avoid negative values in the minutes and second's part. To convert the -13\' to positive minutes, you can borrow 1 degree and add 60 minutes: B=360°-z 360° - 37°13\' = 322°47\'
